data.title: EU trade chief says he held another call with US's Lutnick
data.description:
European Union Trade Commissioner Maros Sefcovic said on Friday that he had held another call with U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, as the EU and the United States continue efforts to reach a deal on tariffs.

"Another call with U.S. Secretary @howardlutnick. Our time and effort fully invested, as delivering forward-looking solutions remains a top EU priority. Staying in permanent contact," wrote Sefcovic on X.

The EU wants to see an end to 25% tariffs on steel and cars and for Trump to drop his so-called "reciprocal" tariff, which was provisionally set at 20% for the EU but is being held at 10% during a 90-day pause until July.

Sefcovic said earlier this week in Dubai that the European Commission was in talks with Washington on possible cooperation in sectors such as aerospace, steel, semiconductors and critical minerals.

data.title: Italian Annual Inflation Rate Declines to 1.7% in May
data.description:
Italy's annual inflation rate declined to 1.7% in May from 1.9% in April, preliminary data from statistics agency Istat showed Friday.

The initial figure is consistent with the consensus estimate, Trading Economics and Investing.com reported.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices recorded zero movement, compared with the expected and prior 0.1% uptick.

The Italian harmonized inflation rate came in at 1.9% on an annual basis, down from the previous month's 2% and in line with forecasts. Month over month, harmonized consumer prices inched up 0.1%, following a 0.4% increase earlier.

data.title: Foreign investors raise bets that India stock market rally may stall
data.description:
Foreign investors are becoming more cautious about the Indian stock market, indicating a three-month rally may run out of legs despite retail traders growing optimistic, according to monthly derivatives data analysed by two brokerages.

The Nifty 50 has risen about 12% from March through May, largely due to better-than-expected corporate earnings and easing global trade risks. That is nearly double the 6.6% gain in the MSCI Emerging Markets index (.dMIEF00000PUS) in that time.

Foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) pumped $2.66 billion into Indian equities over that period and cut their short positions on the Nifty. A short seller borrows stock at a higher price betting its value will decline, at which point they buy the stock and pocket the profit.

However, FPIs have started the June derivatives series — which runs from May 30 to June 25 — with about $2 billion in Nifty index futures shorts, the highest since February, according to Nuvama Alternative and Quantitative Research.

In contrast, retail investors and high-net-worth individuals (HNIs), called the client category, turned bullish with long positions worth $1.54 billion on Nifty futures, compared with $546 million in shorts from early May.

"This divergence sets up a potential tug-of-war between institutional caution and retail optimism, and could lead to a brief pause in the market rally in June," said Abhilash Pagaria, head of Nuvama.

Indeed, the Nifty's gains have weakened in each month — from 6.3% in March to 3.5% in April and to about 2% in May.

"Markets appear to be waiting for some concrete cues before turning bullish," said Sriram Velayudhan, VP at IIFL Securities.

Velayudhan expects the Nifty 50 to trade between 24,300 and 25,300 points over the June series, compared with its current level of about 24,800 points.

Analysts expect the Nifty to hit new highs by end-2025, but say a correction is likely in the next three months, according to a Reuters poll.

data.title: Easing trade tensions launch STOXX 600 for monthly advance
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 on track for a monthly advance
  • STOXX 600 up 0.5% on Friday, set weekly gain
  • German retail sales fall unexpectedly in April
  • Sanofi down after Itepekimab fails to meet certain conditions

European shares jumped on Friday, boosted by energy and real estate stocks, and were on track to end the month with robust gains as easing trade tensions revived risk-taking among investors.

The continent-wide STOXX 600 index was up 0.5%, as of 0842 GMT, negating initial pressures from a temporary reinstatement of the most sweeping of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs a day after another court ordered an immediate block on them.

The benchmark index was set for its first monthly advance in three, up 4.4% so far, capitalising on easing trade tensions as well as the recent U.S. fiscal concerns that forced investors to move away from American assets.

Europe's aerospace and defence index (.SXPARO) was the top winning sector for the month, up more than 14%, as dimming hopes of a truce between Russia and Ukraine persuaded investors to buy ammunition stocks.

Also helping the index were European governments ramping up their defence spending in response to deepening geopolitical tensions and Trump's warnings to reduce their military reliance on Washington.

"The plans to build up the European defences and the resulting need for funding by European governments are attracting overseas investors," said Philip Marey, senior U.S. strategist at Rabobank.

A record bull run for the euro zone's equities is expected in 2026, a Reuters Poll showed on Thursday, after a slight increase by the end of 2025.

The main STOXX 600 index was also on pace to end the week in green, primarily boosted by Trump's decision to back away from tariff threats against Europe, opening doors for Brussels to produce a trade deal with Washington.

Real estate (.SX86P) led sectoral gains on Friday, rising more than 1%. The index was also among the top winners for the week, up 2.8%, leveraging a weekly decline in euro zone benchmark Bund yields.

Energy (.SXEP) rose 0.8% for the day and was up 2.3% for the week.

Among individual stocks, M&G jumped 6.1% after it said Japanese life insurer Dai-Ichi Life Holdings will take a 15% stake in the British insurer and asset manager as part of a strategic deal.

French pharmaceutical company Sanofi fell 5.9% to a more than one-year low after its experimental drug Itepekimab failed to meet certain conditions.

Meanwhile, investors looked ahead to Germany's May inflation figures, to be released later in the day, that could offer more clues about the European Central Bank's policy decision next week. Traders widely expect a quarter-point interest rate cut.

Early data showed German retail sales fell by 1.1% in April compared with the previous month.

data.title: Slovenia Inflation Rate Eases to 3-Month Low
data.description:
Slovenia’s annual inflation rate slowed to 1.8% in May 2025 from 2.3% in the previous month.

This marked the lowest reading since December 2024 as prices moderated for food and non-alcoholic beverages (5.5% vs 5.9% in April), alcoholic beverages and tobacco (2.9% vs 3.3%), clothing and footwear (1.3% vs 4.2%).

Additionally, costs declined further for both transport (-1.9% vs -0.1) and housing and utilities (-2.1% vs -1.8%), while prices slipped for communications (-0.5% vs 0.9%).

Meanwhile, prices accelerated for restaurants and hotels (5.3% vs 4.4%), education (4.6% vs 3.5%), health (4.3% vs 4.0%), and recreation and culture (3.1% vs 3.0%).

On a monthly basis, consumer prices stalled in May 2025, compared to a 1.3% increase in the previous month.

data.title: China to Help with Upgrade of Electronic Information Manufacturing Sector
data.description:
The Chinese government aims to digitally transform the electronic information manufacturing industry by setting an 85% goal for numerical control in key production processes by 2027, Xinhua News Agency reported Wednesday.

By 2027, companies are set to attain new information infrastructure to support digital transformation, with a deeper integration of artificial intelligence in industry development, the report said.

The Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and other government agencies say the plan will help the electronic information manufacturing industry become a pioneering sector of China's economy, according to Xinhua.

data.title: ECB's Panetta warns crypto losses can harm confidence in banks
data.description:
Key points:
  • Bank customers may not be fully aware of crypto-asset risks
  • Stablecoins not good payment means without proper regulation
  • Rules alone are not the solution, we need the digital euro

European Central Bank policymaker and Bank of Italy Governor Fabio Panetta called on Friday for close monitoring of the reputational risks banks face in providing crypto-asset services, warning that losses could harm the trust of customers.

Presenting the Bank of Italy's annual report, Panetta warned about the growing links between the world of crypto-assets and the traditional financial system, pointing to the rising number of accords between banks and digital asset providers.

"Crypto-asset holders might not fully understand their nature and conflate them with traditional banking products, with potentially negative repercussions for confidence in the credit system should losses occur," Panetta said.

Italy's biggest bank Intesa Sanpaolo in January carried out what CEO Carlo Messina described as "a test", by buying 1 million euros in bitcoins, the world's largest digital currency.

Intesa set up a proprietary trading desk for digital assets in 2023, and last year started handling spot trades with crypto-assets.

Spain's Santander is weighing a digital asset expansion, including early-stage plans to offer a stablecoin as well as access to cryptocurrencies for retail customers of its digital bank, Bloomberg reported on Thursday.

Panetta said stablecoins, which are designed to maintain a stable value against underlying currencies or assets, posed a threat to traditional means of payments if large foreign-based technology platforms decided to promote their use.

"In the absence of adequate regulation, their suitability as a means of payment is doubtful, to say the least," he said.

The central banker warned, however, that it would be foolish to think that the spread of crypto-assets, including stablecoins, can be curbed simply by imposing restrictions.

"What is needed is a response that matches the ongoing technological transformation," he said, adding that "the digital euro project stems precisely from this need."

The European Central Bank is working to develop a digital currency to compete with private alternatives that risk undermining the role of central bank money.

data.title: China signals softer stance on rare earth export curbs
data.description:
China said it would cooperate further with other countries over its rare earth export controls as shortages put auto and semiconductor makers in Europe and India at risk of closure.

China, which controls over 90% of global processing capacity for the rare earth magnets used in everything from automobiles and fighter jets to home appliances, imposed restrictions in early April requiring exporters to obtain licences from Beijing.

While a handful of licences have been granted, including to some Volkswagen suppliers, Indian automakers say they have received none and will have to stop production in early June.

"We stand ready to strengthen dialogue and cooperation in the field of export control with relevant countries and regions and stay committed to maintaining the stability of global production and supply chains," foreign ministry spokesperson Lin Jian said on Friday when asked about the controls.

Chinese state media reported on Wednesday that the country could relax its curbs on rare earths exports for Chinese and European semiconductor firms after meetings between industry and the Ministry of Commerce where the issue of shortages was raised.

The New York Times reported earlier this week that the United States suspended some sales of critical technologies to China, including parts for state-owned plane maker COMAC, in response to China's restriction on exports of critical minerals.

data.title: Pihlajalinna Cuts Revenue Outlook For 2025
data.description:
Pihlajalinna Oyj :

  • PROFIT WARNING: PIHLAJALINNA UPDATES ITS GUIDANCE FOR 2025

  • IS LOWERING ITS REVENUE OUTLOOK FOR 2025, PRIMARILY DUE TO EARLIER-THAN-EXPECTED TRANSFER OF SERVICES TO WELLBEING SERVICES COUNTY AND DIVESTMENT OF SPECIAL HOUSING SERVICES

  • CONFIRMS ITS PROFITABILITY GUIDANCE (ADJUSTED EBITA) OF AT LEAST EUR 65 MILLION (EUR 55.2 MILLION IN 2024)

  • ESTIMATES 2025 REVENUE TO FALL SLIGHTLY BELOW PREVIOUS YEAR'S LEVEL (EUR 704.4 MILLION IN 2024)

  • ESTIMATES 2025 ADJUSTED EBITA INCREASE TO AT LEAST EUR 65 MILLION (EUR 55.2 MILLION IN 2024)

  • ESTIMATES DEMAND TO REMAIN STABLE

  • SLOW ECONOMIC GROWTH MAY AFFECT PIHLAJALINNA'S SERVICE DEMAND AND FINANCIAL RESULT MORE THAN EXPECTED

  • PREVIOUS GUIDANCE: GROUP ESTIMATES CONSOLIDATED REVENUE TO REMAIN ON A PAR WITH PREVIOUS YEAR'S LEVEL (EUR 704.4 MILLION IN 2024)

  • PREVIOUS GUIDANCE: ESTIMATES ADJUSTED OPERATING PROFIT BEFORE AMORTISATION AND IMPAIRMENT OF INTANGIBLE ASSETS (EBITA) TO INCREASE TO AT LEAST 9 PER CENT OF REVENUE (7.8 PER CENT IN 2024)

data.title: Chinese students face anxious wait for visas under US crackdown
data.description:
Key points:
  • State Department accused China of exploiting US universities
  • Chinese state media calls visa measures 'educational witch-hunt'
  • Sign of latest spillover from US-China trade war tensions

Caught in the middle of Washington's renewed visa crackdown on Chinese international students, Beijing postgraduate Lainey is anxiously waiting to resume the visa process to study a PhD at her dream school, the University of California.

"We feel helpless and unable to do anything," said the 24-year-old sociology student, who declined to give her surname for privacy reasons.

"The situation in North America this year is not very good. From applying for my PhD until now, this series of visa policies is not very favourable to us. But we have no choice but to wait."

The U.S. State Department said on Thursday it would not tolerate the "exploitation" of American universities or theft of U.S. research and intellectual property by Beijing. Spokesperson Tammy Bruce did not elaborate on how many Chinese students would be affected by a new plan announced on Wednesday to "aggressively" revoke visas.

The visa crackdown is the latest in a series of moves targeting the international student community, especially Chinese nationals, who make up roughly 1 in 4 of all international students in the U.S., as the Trump administration pursues its hardline immigration agenda.

If applied to a broad segment of the 277,000 Chinese students already at U.S. colleges, the visa revocations could disrupt a major source of income for universities and a crucial pipeline of talent for U.S. technology companies.

Chinese students make up 16% of all graduate science, technology, engineering and maths (STEM) students in the United States.

DEFER ENROLMENT?

The announcement on Chinese student visa holders came after the Trump administration ordered its missions worldwide to stop scheduling new appointments for student and exchange visitor visa applicants.

If the visa appointment system is not resumed soon, Lainey wishes to defer enrolment for a year.

"Although everyone says the U.S. admissions system may be biased against Chinese students, in reality U.S. schools are indeed the top in terms of academic quality," she said.

"I may also consider (applying to) some places outside the U.S., such as Europe, as well as Hong Kong and Singapore."

The measures are a sign of the increasing spillover from a bruising trade war between the two global superpowers, and threaten to derail a fragile truce reached mid-May in Geneva.

A Friday editorial by China's state-owned Global Times newspaper said the new visa measures raised "the spectre of McCarthyism" and likened them to an "educational witch-hunt".

"In recent years, the suppression of Chinese students has increasingly become an important part of the U.S. strategy to contain China," the commentary said.

Potentially even more damaging than the immediate economic impact for the U.S. could be a long-term erosion of the appeal of U.S. universities and the subsequent brain drain.

International students - 54% of them from India and China - contributed more than $50 billion to the U.S. economy in 2023, according to the U.S. Department of Commerce.

"If I really have to wait until 2026 to reapply, I might not have such positive feelings towards America," said Lainey.

"If I can't even get a visa, then I'd have no choice but to go somewhere else."

data.title: Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) contract prices inch lower in Europe
data.description: This week, PVC prices slipped in the European region.An industry source in Europe informed a Polymerupdate team member, "The European polyvinyl chloride market experienced restricted spot demand during the week, as market players focused on ethylene MCP for June due to low seasonal demand. Concurrently, discussions over May contract prices between buyers and European polyvinyl chloride (PVC) manufacturers are nearing completion. Discussions focused on reductions due to lower feedstock costs, while manufacturers sought to enhance margins by restricting the size of price declines. Offers from Asia remain below the threshold that local manufacturers can offer, restricting spot transactions. Maintenance turnarounds are planned to conclude by the end of next month, which may extend PVC availability in the area. A local producer has resumed operations at its facility in Pernis, the Netherlands. Market activity is constrained due to weak demand and ongoing uncertainty regarding US tariffs. US tariffs and Chinese counter-tariffs have disturbed global trade flows, resulting in a rise in low Asian offers in Europe and setting a limit on domestic prices. The source added, "Many market participants noted that demand was slightly stronger in May than in April, which was linked to a decrease in national holidays across Europe. Some buyers were reportedly adopting a wait-and-see approach regarding their purchase quantities. Nonetheless, certain sources with clients primarily in the window profile and pipe sectors indicated that purchasing interest was a bit slower than in April. May has not seen the expected seasonal demand from the construction sector, and there's mixed sentiment regarding the level of this demand for June, which is usually a robust month for PVC consumption. In the downstream piping and window framing sector, sources indicated that there was fierce rivalry among manufacturers. Producers have observed that demand this year has not been as weak as anticipated, holding a somewhat more positive outlook than what some other market players have mentioned, but the overall scenario remains subdued. Buying interest in the spot market was stronger in Poland compared to other regions in Europe. Supply in Europe continues to be adequate. A facility in Germany continues with scheduled maintenance for PVC manufacturing. Although production at facilities in Poland and France had recently experienced disruptions, they were planning to resume operations by the end of May. In the contract markets, PVC Suspension grade prices were assessed at the Euro 955-960/mt FD NWE Germany and FD NWE France levels, both dropped by Euro (-5/mt) from last week. PVC Suspension grade prices were assessed at the Euro 965-970/mt FD NWE Italy levels, a marginal fall of Euro (-5/mt) from the previous week. Meanwhile, PVC prices were assessed at the GBP 800-805/mt FD NWE UK levels, a decrease of GBP (-10/mt) from week on week.In the spot markets, FD North West Europe PVC prices were assessed at the Euro 875-885/mt levels, steady from last week. The source stated, "Asian materials keep arriving in Europe, with a changing perspective from certain smaller converters regarding their acceptance. It was reported that materials from China were being offered at the most competitive rates. Producers will remain eager to restore margins in June, as demand for PVC typically reaches its peak in the upcoming months. Market players focused on the ethylene industry-settled contract price for June. Producers noted the necessity to enhance their margins amid any shift in ethylene, pointing to present producer margins as unviable. The market monitored closely the progress in peace talks between Russia and Ukraine, as a resolution could result in heightened demand for reconstruction activities. In other regions, a fluctuating tariff environment was noted to reduce demand by fostering economic instability. In the meantime, E-PVC demand stays weak, the automotive industry is sluggish in Europe, and although exported quantities offer some optimism, ongoing tariff disputes from the US have caused concern among certain domestic producers in Europe. The US has currently imposed a 10% tariff on e-PVC imports from the European Union, but there was a potential escalation on Friday when President Trump proposed a universal 50% tariff on European exports to the US starting June 1. The tariff proposal may serve as an additional negotiation strategy to facilitate a deal, yet in the near term, the ambiguity will pose difficulties for everyone in terms of planning and management. The US has restricted domestic production of e-PVC, leading to a dependence on imports. It was suggested that American buyers will keep acquiring e-PVC from Europe since alternatives are quite limited, though 50% tariffs would pose challenges.In Turkey, market activity declined as Eid al-Adha drew near. European producers maintained a limited desire to export to Turkey at the current price points, due to competitive offers from other nations. Certain offers from China were withdrawn due to anticipated rises in shipping costs from Asia to TurkeyMeanwhile, feedstock EDC prices were assessed at the USD 155-165/mt FOB North West Europe levels while VCM prices were assessed at the USD 495-505/mt FOB North West Europe levels, both stable from last week.Ethylene spot prices on Thursday were assessed at the Euro 670-780/mt FD North West Europe levels, a decline of Euro (-25/mt) from the previous week.European Ethylene contract price for June 2025 settled at the Euro 1135/MT FD North West Europe levels. This price represents a roll over from its May 2025 settlement levels.

data.title: India's Bharat Electronics set for best month in one year
data.description:
** India's Bharat Electronics jumps 23.2% in May, set for its best monthly gain in a year

** Stock also set for its eighth straight week of gains

** Gains in-line with other defence stocks which rose amid India-Pakistan border clash and Prime Minister Narendra Modi's push for indigenous defence equipment

** BAJE's gains also boosted by its upbeat fourth-quarter results which highlighted its strong order pipeline

** BAJE's inclusion in BSE Sensex , effective June 23, also drove stock higher

** Stock extends YTD gains to 32%

data.title: EU gains leverage in trade talks as US court casts doubt on tariffs, EU officials say
data.description:
Key points:
  • EU gains leverage in trade talks after U.S. court ruling
  • EU sticks to zero tariffs offer despite U.S. court uncertainty
  • EU sees positive aspect in chaotic U.S. tariff policy

The European Union has gained leverage in trade talks with the United States after a U.S. court cast doubt on the legality of Washington's "reciprocal" tariffs, EU officials said on Friday.

The European Commission said trade talks between Brussels and Washington would continue, with Europe sticking to its offer of mutual zero tariffs on industrial goods.

"There's no change in our approach, we proceed as planned with both technical and political meetings next week," a Commission spokesperson said.

A U.S. federal appeals court temporarily reinstated President Donald Trump's tariffs on Thursday, a day after a U.S. trade court ruled that Trump had exceeded his authority in imposing the duties and ordered an immediate block on them.

"The uncertainty as to the legality of the 'reciprocal' tariffs certainly gives us extra leverage," one EU official close to the talks said. "The talks will continue, as formally we still look for zero-for-zero tariffs," the official said.

The EU was also willing to discuss some non-trade barriers with the U.S., officials said, but would not touch the EU's taxation system — such as the value added tax or digital tax — or food safety standards.

The Commission, which conducts all trade negotiations on behalf of the 27-nation bloc, would not comment on the rulings because they were internal U.S. procedures.

EU Trade Commissioner Maros Sefcovic in a post on the X social media platform said he held a phone call with U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick on Friday.

"Our time and effort fully invested, as delivering forward-looking solutions remains a top EU priority. Staying in permanent contact," Sefcovic said on X.

More trade talks between the U.S. and the EU are scheduled for next week, on the sidelines of the OECD Ministerial Council Meeting in Paris on June 3-4.

EU officials said the U.S. courts' rulings validated the EU view that the sweeping "reciprocal" tariffs, imposed on all goods from the EU and many other countries around the world on April 2, were unjustified.

The officials also said that while U.S. courts did not question Washington's 25% tariffs imposed on European steel, aluminium and cars, the rulings could also play a role in the EU's efforts to get those tariffs lowered or removed.

They also said the uncertainty created by the court rulings and the Trump administration's tariff policy also had a positive aspect for Europe, which was seen by markets as an oasis of stability in comparison.

"This is the watchword: uncertainty. It is impossible to know what the status of the tariffs will be next week, not to mention next month!" the EU official said.

"If you want sane, stable, even boring, rules-based order and predictable business environment, Europe is the place for you!" the official said.

Daily performance is calculated by reference to the previous day NAV/share and VNAS (Vietnam All Share Index). Calendar month-to-date performance is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 29 Apr 2025. Calendar year-to-date performance is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS at the previous calendar year end (31 Dec 2024). 1-year performance is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 30 May 2024. 3-year CAGR (compound annual growth rate) is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 30 May 2022. 5-year CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 30 May 2020. 10-year CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 30 May 2015. 15-year CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 30 May 2010. Since inception CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share as of 30 Jun 2006. VNAS is sourced from Bloomberg, which uses back-tested positions for VNAS for before 27 Jan 2014 as released by HoSE (Ho Chi Minh City Stock Exchange).

The Estimated Daily NAV stated in this announcement is based on estimated valuations and accordingly the Company accepts no responsibility for the accuracy of the Estimated Daily NAV figure given, and neither is any responsibility implied. The actual month end NAV in any month may vary from the Estimated Daily NAV provided in the relevant month. Accordingly, no reliance should be placed on the Estimated Daily NAV, and it has been provided for indicative purposes only.

data.title: Valereum Signs Digital Asset MoU with US-based Blubird
data.description:
Valereum (VLRM.AQ) signed a digital asset-focused memorandum of understanding with US-based software-as-a-service group Blubird Global, according to a Friday release.

The financial technology company will secure a 10-year license to Blubird's Web3 Founders Tools suite. The parties also plan to initially list up to $2 billion of Blubird's more than $55 billion in tokenized assets on Valereum's marketplace, subject to issuer consent as well as technical and regulatory clearances.

The companies may also pursue potential joint initiatives as Valereum looks to expand its digital asset ecosystem and advance its monetization strategy and global growth.

data.title: Mipco Seamless Rings (Gujarat) Gets Board Nod for Name Change
data.description:
Mipco Seamless Rings (Gujarat) has received board approval to change the company's name to Mipco, according to a Thursday filing to the BSE.

The proposal is subject to the approval of the members and the applicable regulatory authorities.

data.title: Polish CPI in May lower than expected, boosts rate cut bets
data.description:
Inflation in Poland was slightly lower than expected in May, official data showed on Friday, strengthening market expectations that the central bank's Monetary Policy Council (MPC) will cut interest rates, possibly in July.

Annual inflation in May was 4.1%, according to a flash estimate from the statistics office, just below the 4.2% forecast in a Reuters poll, and easing from 4.3% in March. The central bank's inflation target is 1.5-3.5%

Month-on-month inflation fell by 0.2% in April, while analysts had expected an increase of 0.1%.

"The data, combined with the reduction in gas tariffs, confirms that CPI inflation will fall to around 3% - a return to the broad inflation target with a bang. Our forecast of a 25 basis point rate cut in July remains valid," PKO BP analysts wrote in a commentary on the data.

Poland's energy market regulator has cut the gas prices that wholesaler PGNiG Obrot Detaliczny charges to households and vulnerable users including hospitals and schools by 14.8%.

Finance minister Andrzej Domanski wrote on social media platform X that lower tariffs "will lower the inflation path in the coming months".

The MPC lowered the cost of credit by 50 basis points in May, making its first cut since October 2023, citing slowing inflation, weakening wage growth and worsening economic conditions. The main interest rate is currently 5.25%.

National Bank of Poland Governor Adam Glapinski said earlier this month that the fact that the bank had cut rates did not mean a cycle of monetary policy easing was beginning.

Some members of the MPC indicated that July, when new inflation forecasts will be published, may be a good time to start discussions on further interest rate cuts, which could take place later this year.
